shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the moon full over the lake, its cold lane laid across the water

segments.text: Of all the lights that cross the garden's roof, only one is a world in its own right. The moon is Earth's single natural satellite — a ball of rock roughly a quarter of our planet's width, close enough that we see it as a disc and not a star. It makes no light of its own. Every beam it lays across this water is sunlight, borrowed and thrown back.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the pale disc alone in frame, craters catching the sun's borrowed light

segments.text: Look closely and the surface is a record of violence. Those dark plains and bright rings are impact craters, some billions of years old — and because the moon has almost no air and no weather, nothing has ever wiped them away. The moon itself was likely born from a collision: a body the size of a small planet struck the young Earth, and the flung debris gathered into the companion we see tonight.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: from the bright disc above down its reflection to the swelling lake

segments.text: But the moon does more than decorate the dark. It reaches down and pulls. This is the story of that pull — the moon, and the tides.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: the whole lake from above, its outline subtly but visibly breathing

segments.text: Gravity weakens with distance, so the moon tugs the near side of a body of water slightly harder than the far side. Water is free to move, and so it heaps up — not once but twice, into two bulges: one swelling toward the moon, and one on the exact opposite side, left behind as the moon pulls the planet out from under it.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: macro at the waterline: water climbing blade by blade into the grass margin

segments.text: Then the world turns. As Earth rotates through those two standing bulges, most coasts sweep through two high waters and two low waters every day. The sun adds its own pull — about a third as strong as the moon's — but it is the moon that sets the beat. High, low, high, low: the oldest schedule there is.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: the tide line drawn on the sand: wet dark below, dry pale above

segments.text: And it drifts. Each day the tide arrives about fifty minutes later than the last, because the moon has moved a little further along its month-long orbit, and the water is chasing a target that will not hold still.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: following the moon's arc across the dome in accelerated night

segments.text: Watch across a month and the tides breathe with a slower rhythm. When the sun, Earth, and moon fall into a line — at new moon and at full — their two pulls add together. The water climbs highest and falls lowest. Sailors named these the spring tides, and the name has nothing to do with the season.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: split mood: one enormous tide flooding the margins, then a timid one

segments.text: A week later the moon stands at a right angle to the sun, and the two pulls work against each other. The tides go slack and shallow — the neap tides, when high water barely rises and low water barely falls. Twice a month strong, twice a month weak: a slow envelope wrapped around the daily pulse.

shot.subject: fish
shot.notes: fish gliding over newly drowned meadow at high water

segments.text: Life reads this clock. On our own world, whole reefs of coral spawn together on a single full moon; fish and crabs run their tides to the minute; countless creatures shift their hunting and hiding between bright nights and dark ones, because a full moon can mean either a hunter's advantage or a prey's danger.

shot.subject: ant
shot.notes: ants working the freshly exposed wet zone at low water

segments.text: Even navigation runs on moonlight. Some insects steer by holding a fixed angle to the moon the way a sailor holds a star — dung beetles rolling a straight line by lunar light, moths keeping their bearing against it. Take the moon away, and a surprising amount of the living world would lose its timing and its compass at once.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: the wet band between high and low water: dark, glistening, printed with traffic

segments.text: Between the highest reach of the water and its lowest retreat lies the richest, harshest ground of all — the intertidal zone, drowned and bared twice a day. On sea coasts, life sorts itself into strict bands here: the animals that can bear long hours in the air live high, and those that need the water live low, each species pinned to the exact stripe of shore it can survive.

shot.subject: shore
shot.notes: low water: ants working the exposed band while it lasts, moving quickly

segments.text: Watch the garden's own margin and you can read the same law in miniature. At low water the exposed band is briefly walkable — damp, rich, and busy — and the residents that work it move fast, because the ground keeps a schedule and the schedule grants no extensions.

shot.subject: underwater
shot.notes: high water: fish gleaning the same band from the other side, methodical

segments.text: Hours later the water returns and the fish glean the very same strip from the other side. Two nations, one field, a wall of time where a wall of space should be — and the tideline fed by both.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the moon high and small over the whole breathing world

segments.text: The moon does one more thing, larger than any tide. Its steady pull holds Earth's tilt nearly fixed at about twenty-three degrees — and that fixed tilt is why the seasons keep their order year after year. Without the moon to steady it, the axis would wander, and the climate would lurch between extremes over the ages.

shot.subject: moon
shot.notes: the pale disc alone in frame, silent

segments.text: And it is leaving. The same tidal friction that raises these waters is nudging the moon a few centimeters farther away each year, and slowing our spin — so Earth's day is very slowly lengthening. The nights were shorter, and the moon larger, when the world was young.

shot.subject: water
shot.notes: moonlight on the swelling lake, the beach drinking the advancing edge

segments.text: The ants will never suspect it. The fish will never thank it. Even the Visitor, who might work it out, has beaches to walk. But below, on schedule, the water stands up again to follow its distant clock around the world.

shot.subject: sky
shot.notes: clouds slide across the moon; the silver lane on the lake goes out

segments.text: But the sky does not always keep such gentle company. There are nights when the roof forgets its manners entirely — when the window closes, and the dome turns to noise and fire.

shot.subject: lightning
shot.notes: a bolt tears the dark; one violent frame of white

segments.text: Rain, snow, and the sky's own artillery. Next: the Weather.
